planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early fall, 2–3 inches deep, with the growing tip pointing up; handle carefully as divisions are slow to recover.

directions: Plant in deep, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil beneath a deciduous canopy where leaf litter accumulates naturally; trilliums are adapted to the nutrient cycling of the eastern forest floor and resent disturbance once established. The plant emerges, blooms, and sets seed all within about six weeks in spring before dying back completely to the ground — mark the location carefully so the dormant rhizome is not accidentally disturbed. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants, as wild-collected trilliums are protected in many states and transplant very poorly. Division for propagation should be attempted only on large, multi-year clumps, as the plant needs 7–10 years from a small piece to reach flowering size.

planting.note: Plant corms 2–4 inches deep in fall, flat side down; the corm size determines whether the plant produces a male or female flower — larger corms become female and produce bright-red berry clusters in fall.

directions: Plant corms in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full to partial shade where seasonal moisture is available in spring but soil drains by midsummer. The distinctive hooded spathe (the pulpit) surrounding the upright spadix (Jack) appears in spring, followed by showy clusters of red berries on female plants in late summer. All parts of the plant contain calcium oxalate crystals and are highly irritating if eaten raw; extensive traditional preparation was required by Indigenous peoples who used the corm as food. The plant dies back completely by midsummer; underplant with hostas or ferns to cover the gap.

planting.note: Plant rhizome sections 2 inches deep in early spring; the rhizome spreads aggressively underground to form colonies — give it ample space in a naturalized area.

directions: Plant in moist, rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; this woodland native spreads by horizontal rhizomes to form dramatic colonies of large umbrella-like leaves that carpet the forest floor. The single white flower hidden beneath the paired leaves in spring develops into a yellowish, lemon-scented fruit by midsummer — the only part of the plant that is edible when fully soft and ripe. The roots, leaves, and unripe fruit contain podophyllin, a potent toxin, and should never be consumed. The plant goes dormant and disappears by late summer, so interplant with ferns or other shade perennials to fill the void.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in fall in groups of 12 or more for a natural effect; flowering takes several years from a small corm.

directions: Plant small corms in well-drained, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees where they will receive spring sunlight before canopy leafout and remain cool and moist through early summer. The distinctive mottled (trout-marked) foliage appears first; plants typically produce only a single pair of leaves for several years before reaching the size needed to bloom. Once established, colonies spread slowly by underground stolons and seed to naturalize beautifully. The leaves are edible raw or cooked with a cucumber-like flavor, and the small corm is edible but harvesting kills the plant — admire rather than harvest from established colonies.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes after foliage dies back in summer; plant horizontally about 2 inches deep in fall, keeping the red-sap-bearing rhizome intact.

directions: Plant in slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil beneath deciduous trees; the pure white flowers with golden stamens open for only a few days in very early spring before the leaves expand, making this a treasured harbinger of the season. The rhizome contains brilliant red-orange sap (sanguinarine) that was used historically as a dye and in traditional medicine, but is toxic if ingested and irritating to skin. Plants go completely dormant by midsummer, so interplant with ferns or hostas that emerge later to fill the space. Divide established clumps every few years in late summer when dormant, cutting rhizomes into sections each with at least one growing tip.
